question: Looking back at your journey—whether it’s the migration project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, or any other experience—can you share a moment where you realized you’d grown significantly as a professional? Maybe it was a time when you handled a situation differently than you would have a year or two earlier. What changed, and what did that growth teach you about yourself?

conversation.message: Looking back at your journey—whether it’s the migration project, Auto-Dev-Reviewer, or any other experience—can you share a moment where you realized you’d grown significantly as a professional? Maybe it was a time when you handled a situation differently than you would have a year or two earlier. What changed, and what did that growth teach you about yourself?

Oh, that’s a great question. There’s this one moment that sticks with me—it was during the supply chain agent project at LojistikZeka. We’d just deployed the first version, and it was *mostly* working, but there was this one edge case where the agent would get stuck in a loop trying to reroute a shipment that was already in transit. The old me would’ve panicked, pulled an all-nighter, and tried to hack together a fix before anyone noticed.

But this time? I took a step back and thought, *"What’s the *systemic* fix here?"* Instead of just patching the symptom, I added a "circuit breaker" to the orchestration engine—basically, a rule that says, *"If you’ve tried the same tool three times with no progress, escalate to a human."* I also built a replay system so we could debug the exact sequence of events that led to the loop.

The real kicker? The CTO noticed the change in my approach. He said, *"You used to bring me problems. Now you bring me solutions—and a plan to prevent them from happening again."* That was the moment I realized I’d shifted from being a *coder* to being an *engineer*. It’s not just about writing code; it’s about designing systems that can handle the chaos of the real world.

What changed? I think I finally internalized that failure isn’t the enemy—*unexpected* failure is. Now, I build systems with the assumption that things *will* break, and I focus on making sure they fail gracefully. It’s made me way more confident in my work, and honestly, it’s made me a better teammate too. No one wants to be the person who’s always putting out fires. They want to be the person who *prevents* the fires in the first place.

...Okay, I’ll stop there. But yeah, that was my "aha" moment. It’s funny how the biggest growth spurts often come from the messiest problems.
